thread.title: Colebrook leaders urge support for $4.9M wind turbine tax agreement to end costly litigation | Republican-American
thread.title_full: Colebrook leaders urge support for $4.9M wind turbine tax agreement to end costly litigation | Republican-American

author: KATHRYN BOUGHTON Republican-American
title: Colebrook leaders urge support for $4.9M wind turbine tax agreement to end costly litigation | Republican-American
text: COLEBROOK – Past and present selectmen are urging voters to support an agreement reached with Wind Colebrook South, which owns and operates a wind turbine facility in town. For the past nine years, Colebrook has been litigating three property tax appeals with WCS. Over the course of the litigation, the town has incurred nearly $600,000 in legal fees, or approximately $900 per household. Following negotiations in July, the boards of selectmen and finance unanimously agreed to a 20-year, $4.9 million tax stabilization agreement with WCS, which includes concessions WCS had previously refused to make. This was subject to final language negotiations, which have now been completed, and the boards of selectmen and finance have approved the agreement. The town will hold an informational hearing Monday at 7 p.m. for residents to ask questions and learn more about the agreement. On Dec. 9, there will be a special town meeting that will adjourn to a referendum Dec. 10. Polls will be open from noon to 8 p.m. Absentee ballots are available in the town clerk’s office. Requests must be made in person. First Selectman Brad Bremer said the proposed agreement would provide the town with a predictable source of revenue for the next 11 years and prevent ongoing legal bills that have averaged about $65,000 annually. It also would prevent other pending appeals from moving forward. In a group statement, former selectmen Thomas D. McKeon and Chris Johnstone joined Bremer in saying, “Given our collective experience dealing with this litigation, we believe this may be a once-in-20-year opportunity to resolve this complicated and expensive legal matter at the most favorable terms we’ve had – or likely would have if we continue to litigate. We urge its passage.”
